texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The increase of carbon dioxide concentration in atmosphere due to fossil fuel usage, and its implications on global warming, are now commonly admitted [1]. CO2 concentration is expected to double around 2050, if no special actions are taken. Among the different options that can prevent build-up (improved energy efficiency, increased use of renewable or non CO2 emitting energy sources), carbon capture and storage (CCS) is considered as a key issue [2]. Nevertheless, the recovery, transport and geological storage of CO2, in priority from large emission sources, are a formidable scientific and technological challenge. More specifically, the identification of the most efficient capture process, which would enable to recover 80-90% of the CO2 from a given source, has attracted considerable attention for several years, due to the fact that this step concentrates 60-80% of the overall CCS cost, both in terms of capital (CAPEX) and operating (OPEX) costs [3,4]. A very large number of capture processes has been already explored and, basically, three main strategies, summarized in Table 1 can be proposed [5]:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The possible place and role of different membrane processes for post combustion carbon dioxide capture are reviewed and discussed. In a first step, the interest and limitations of membrane contactors in order to achieve intensified CO2 absorption in a chemical solvent are briefly exposed. Membrane based separation processes are presented and discussed in a second step, with two major strategies: low selectivity, high permeability materials which require multistage processes, and chemically reactive, high selectivity membrane materials which could possibly achieve the target CO2 capture process in a single stage. A special emphasis is put on materials and design challenges. Unexplored issues (target CO2 purity, cost curves, hybrid processes, role of water, minor components) are finally exposed as major research targets.

texts.orig: · For the last option, namely the postcombustion approach, the flue gas is directly treated after the combustion step. A CO2 capture process is designed in order to concentrate and purify carbon dioxide up to the target specifications for transportation and storage. This strategy can be applied in principle to any existing plant where a CO2 containing stream is produced (a so called retrofit property, which cannot be achieved by oxycombustion or precombustion options). Furthermore, postcombustion can be proposed not only to power plants like the two previous options, but also to any industrial plant (steel, cement, petrochemical plants, . . . ). These two strong advantages have however to be balanced by the fact that a diluted, low pressure, wet CO2/N2 mixture has to be treated. As a consequence, postcombustion carbon dioxide capture can be anticipated to be an outstanding process design challenge [6].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From a chemical engineering and process design points of view, the identification of the most relevant capture process for postcombustion application requires before all a clear definition of the composition of the mixture to be treated (inlet boundary conditions), together with the target specifications, namely the CO2 purity and maximal tolerable range of concentrations of the different species of the feed mixture (outlet boundary conditions). Surprisingly, these two key set of data are not always well defined and/or taken into account for CCS studies:
texts.text: From a chemical engineering and process design points of view, the identification of the most relevant capture process for postcombustion application requires before all a clear definition of the composition of the mixture to be treated (inlet boundary conditions), together with the target specifications, namely the CO2 purity and maximal tolerable range of concentrations of the different species of the feed mixture (outlet boundary conditions). Surprisingly, these two key set of data are not always well defined and/or taken into account for CCS studies:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: - The flue gas has a composition which is far from constant from one source to the other. The CO2 content (volume basis) can be as low as 4% in a gas turbine plant, around 15% for coal power plants, and more concentrated ( ∼ 20-30%) for cement and steel production plants. Larger concentrations can occasionally be found in some special situations such as ammonia, syngas or biofuels (i.e. ethanol fermentation) plants [2]. Additionally, apart from CO2, different compounds can be found; for instance N2, O2, NO x , SO x

In general, the flue gas is wet and saturated humidity conditions can be taken as a rule. Nevertheless, despite these broad ranges of flue gas compositions, the large majority of studies reported in the field of carbon capture process will be performed on dry CO2/N2 mixtures, usually around 15% CO2 content, which corresponds, by far, to the largest part of CO2 emissions type in the world (i.e. coal power plants [6]).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -The clear definition of the capture specifications is also far from being clear and unanimous. The target CO2 purity can be as low as 80% or up to 99% depending on studies and recommendations [7]. This apparently moderate target purity range should not be considered as a detail in terms of capture process selection; for the well known air separation problem, pictured in Fig. 1, different technologies will be chosen if a N2 purity larger than 99.9% (cryogeny), 99% (PSA) or 90% (membranes) is required. One can expect that an even larger variety of capture processes will occur in CCS, given the diversity of situations that can be encountered, compared to air separation. The uncertainty on target CO2 purity is not the only difficulty in the outlet conditions definition; an even fuzzier situation prevails concerning the acceptable range of minor compounds to be found in the concentrated CO2 stream, which will be also governed by transport and storage constraints. These issues remain essentially unexplored in the open literature [7]; similarly to many other separation studies, process design is first explored on a simplified model binary mixture, before the complex real multicomponent problem is tackled.

texts.orig: It can be concluded at this stage that the postcombustion CCS landscape is complex and shows a large diversity of situations and constraints. A generally utilised reference case is defined by a 15 vol.% CO2/N2 flue gas at the inlet (dry, binary mixture), a 90% CO2 capture ratio (noted R in this paper) and a 90% CO2 target purity (noted y in what follows). Unless specified and similarly to the common practice, these data will be taken as the baseline case in this study.

texts.orig: - First achieve a minimal degree of separation towards the flue gas, so that the outlet specifications are respected. This selectivity criterion (usually noted ˛ ) in CCS, can be considered as not too severe compared to other separation problems such as isotopes or enantiomers separations. As a consequence, a great number of separation processes can pass this first selection step (for instance, more than 100 different processes have been considered in this field in the carbon capture project (CCP) consortium [9]).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -In a great number of cases, different set of operating conditions of a given separation process, which lead to the target outlet conditions, can be identified. The process design study has to explore at this stage the interplay between the energy requirement (included in the operating costs, noted OPEX) and the cost of the installation (included in the capital cost, noted CAPEX), for the different set of data. For the CCS problem however, attention is focused to a great extent to the energy requirement first; this priority comes from the specific target of the operation, namely decrease the total CO2 emissions of the plant. Any increased energy requirement of the process will indeed increase the secondary CO2 emissions. The effective avoided CO2 emissions concept, shown in Fig. 3, takes into account the primary and secondary emissions; the key role of the energy efficiency of the process is also stressed through this mode of representation. The secondary CO2 emissions due to the capture process, which correspond to the difference between the two bars, have obviously to be minimized. This explains why the energy efficiency of the capture process is a key issue. Classically a 3.5 GJ (thermal basis) per ton of recovered CO2 is obtained by the first generation of postcombustion capture processes, namely absorption in amine (MEA) solvents [6,10]. A 2 GJ per ton is often considered as a major target in order to achieve acceptable CO2 capture costs [11].

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: - Membrane contactors have been proposed for a long time in order to achieve intensified gas-liquid CO2 absorption in chemical solvents [13]. No special improvement in the energy efficiency of the process can be expected in that case, because this parameter will still be governed by the nature of the solvent and the regeneration conditions. The major interest of membrane contactors concerns the possibility to significantly decrease the size of the installation (and more specifically of the absorption unit), while keeping the separation performances unchanged, a so-called intensification effect [14]. This possibility, which can possibly be associated to a decreased capital cost (CAPEX) of the installation, can be of great interest given the strong constraints on scale-up, volume and footprints for carbon dioxide capture plants discussed above.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -A large number of studies address a completely different challenges, namely replace the traditional post-combustion CO2 capture process (i.e. gas-liquid absorption) by a membrane separation process [15]. In that case, as discussed before (Fig. 2) the selectivity, energy requirement and productivity of the process are of major importance. Three different strategies, logically showing their own advantages and drawbacks, are currently investigated. The first option consists to use a physical membrane separation principle [16]; to some extent, the selectivity limitations will be similar to those of physical solvents for gas-liquid absorption. Dense polymeric membrane materials are most often proposed for this task and a great number of membrane materials have been tested, almost systematically with dry CO2/N2 mixtures [17]. Energy requirement and productivity issues are addressed in a very limited number of publications. The two other options for CO2/N2 mixture separation are based on a chemically reacting system; the difference between these two processes is related to the fact that in one case the reacting species is fixed (such as in fixed site carrier membranes, FSCM), while it is mobile in the second case (liquid membranes, LM). These processes are known, for a long time, to offer the possibility to show in some cases extremely high selectivities [18]; nevertheless, their energy requirement and productivity performances under realistic flue gas treatment conditions remain largely unexplored.
texts.text: -A large number of studies address a completely different challenges, namely replace the traditional post-combustion CO2 capture process (i.e. gas-liquid absorption) by a membrane separation process [15]. In that case, as discussed before (Fig. 2) the selectivity, energy requirement and productivity of the process are of major importance. Three different strategies, logically showing their own advantages and drawbacks, are currently investigated. The first option consists to use a physical membrane separation principle [16]; to some extent, the selectivity limitations will be similar to those of physical solvents for gas-liquid absorption. Dense polymeric membrane materials are most often proposed for this task and a great number of membrane materials have been tested, almost systematically with dry CO2/N2 mixtures [17]. Energy requirement and productivity issues are addressed in a very limited number of publications. The two other options for CO2/N2 mixture separation are based on a chemically reacting system; the difference between these two processes is related to the fact that in one case the reacting species is fixed (such as in fixed site carrier membranes, FSCM), while it is mobile in the second case (liquid membranes, LM). These processes are known, for a long time, to offer the possibility to show in some cases extremely high selectivities [18]; nevertheless, their energy requirement and productivity performances under realistic flue gas treatment conditions remain largely unexplored.

texts.text: 3. Intensified CO2 absorption by membrane contactors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The possibility to significantly intensify gas-liquid absorption processes thanks to membrane contactors has been proposed for a long time and numerous industrial applications are currently operated [19]. Given the strong constraints on the overall footprint of the absorption units in CCS, it is tempting to explore the possibility to decrease the size devoted to the capture unit thanks to this technology [20]. Numerous studies have been indeed reported the last years where dedicated membrane contactors are investigated for CO2 absorption in a chemical solvent (most often MonoEthanolAmine) [21].

texts.orig: A high k m value requires large pores and a high porosity in order to provide bulk diffusion of the gas, at the expense however of a decreasing acceptable transmembrane pressure. From a practical point of view, among the few experimental k m values which are available, a large range of data is obtained (from 10 -5 to 10 -2 m/s [27]). Taking one or the other of these two limits, opposite conclusions will be obtained concerning the interest of membrane contactors for post-combustion CO2 capture. A systematic analysis of the set of conditions to be fulfilled in terms of membrane material ( k m), fiber dimensions (external diameter, thickness) and module packing, in order to ensure a significant intensification of the postcombustion CO2 gas liquid absorption by membrane contactors, is still lacking. This objective should be considered as a priority in order to offer a rapid evaluation of the interest and limitations of the numerous membrane materials, fibers and modules which are continuously reported for this application.

texts.orig: 3.2. Process challenges
texts.text: 3.2. Process challenges

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas absorption experiments with membrane contactors on a long time scale (typically several weeks or months) are very seldom reported. A large effort should be addressed in this direction in order to experimentally prove the stability of absorption performances in a real flue gas environment. Detrimental wetting effects, due to complex phenomena such as structural changes of the membrane material (swelling by gradual solvent incorporation, mechanical deformation), membrane surface modifications or uncontrolled pressure overshoots, have been effectively occasionally reported [28]. The intensity of these undesired effects depends to a large extent on the type of membrane and solvent which are used. PTFE is usually considered as the most stable material, while unfavorable changes have been reported in particular with PP membranes when exposed to MEA solutions for several weeks [27].

A large effort should be addressed in this direction in order to experimentally prove the stability of absorption performances in a real flue gas environment. Detrimental wetting effects, due to complex phenomena such as structural changes of the membrane material (swelling by gradual solvent incorporation, mechanical deformation), membrane surface modifications or uncontrolled pressure overshoots, have been effectively occasionally reported [28]. The intensity of these undesired effects depends to a large extent on the type of membrane and solvent which are used. PTFE is usually considered as the most stable material, while unfavorable changes have been reported in particular with PP membranes when exposed to MEA solutions for several weeks [27].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Moreover, the very large majority of results are obtained on lab scale membrane contactors, with operating conditions which do not necessarily reproduce industrial operation; for instance, fresh solvent is often used at the inlet liquid side, and the outlet solvent loading is not always controlled. It is important to stress that the real mass transfer performances can hardly be determined under these conditions. In fact, in a real post-combustion application, the inlet solvent will be partly loaded (typically ˛ ∼ 0.25 for MEA) and the outlet solvent loading will be close to saturation (i.e. ˛ ∼ 0.48 for MEA) [29]. Thus, the effective driving force for CO2 absorption is not rigorously reproduced for laboratory scale results which do not respect these boundary conditions. As a consequence, the intensification effect which can be effectively achieved by a membrane contactor, in place of a packed column, remains unclear. Intensification factors as high as 8 have been reported [20]; nevertheless, for other studies, the a posteriori determination of the productivity of the membrane contactor absorption unit can lead to capacities lower than the packed column performances [30]. A rational evaluation of the set of conditions (membrane mass transfer coefficient, membrane dimensions, module design, . . . ) which guarantee a significant intensification effect for a membrane contactor compared to packed columns is clearly necessary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performances of membrane contactors at pilot scale, ideally with real flue gas mixtures, should be rigorously investigated. This is another field of crucial importance because the achievement of maximal mass transfer performances in a membrane contactor demands hydrodynamic dispersions effects (both in the liquid and gas phases), to be controlled. Should the hydrodynamic conditions show a significant departure from plug flow (which can be rather easily satisfied for lab scale modules), a significant decrease of the intensification factor would result. For membrane modules consisting of several thousands of fibers, it might be that performances are less attractive than with small units, due to detrimental hydrodynamic dispersion phenomena at large scale.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, a rigorous comparison of carbon captures costs between a classical packed column and membrane contactor absorption is still lacking. The large difference between packed columns, which provide an economy of scale, and membrane contactors which are governed by a numbering up cost behavior, is certainly a complicating factor. Moreover, few data, if any, of module capital costs are available for membrane contactors. The difference in operating conditions (solvent flowrate, pressure drop), solvent losses, lifetime of the processes have also to be considered in a rigorous technicoeconomical analysis. Generally speaking, these different issues are almost not documented up to now and they can be considered as to have to be investigated in order to evaluate the real impact of membrane contactors in a post-combustion framework.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Overview
texts.text: 4.1. Overview

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the previous section, the association of non selective membranes to a chemical solvent through a hybrid process called membrane contactor, for process intensification purposes, has been discussed. Another possible and more direct role of membrane processes is to replace the absorption unit and achieve the CO2/N2 separation task. A separation effect has obviously to occur in this case. It should be noted first that, in a great number of cases, membrane processes are disgarded in the early stages of process selection for post-combustion CO2 capture. Different types of arguments are usually proposed in order to explain this choice. We propose hereafter a list of the most common limitations of membrane processes which have been occasionally presented (in italics), together with a tentative answer or comment. The objective is to draw a more balanced framework for membranes in a context of capture process selection in post-combustion.

texts.orig: -Membrane processes cannot compete for high flowrate applications. It is right that membrane processes do not offer economy of scale; conversely, they offer a modularity which can be of great interest. Moreover, the engineering challenge related to scaling up membrane processes to 1 million m 3 /h and more has to be stressed. Nevertheless, very large capacities are already operating with membranes for gas separation applications, especially for natural gas treatment. Similarly, the huge flowrates which have to be treated in post-combustion (such as power plants), cannot be treated in a single unit by other processes (for example, for a 600 MW power plant, at least two absorption units are needed for post-combustion amine washing. Similarly, two cryogenic trains would be needed for oxycombustion).

texts.orig: -Membrane processes require high quality energy (namely electricity). First, this specificity can be taken into account in the energy requirement analysis (i.e. in place of a target energy requirement of 2 GJ/ton for heat regenerated processes, 0.5-0.7 GJ/ton only will be allowed for membranes). Second, other processes which require electricity, such as pressure swing adsorption (PSA), have been selected for blast furnace post-combustion carbon capture. Thus, a non thermal energy based separation process is not necessarily inadapted for carbon capture.

The large flexibility for designing membrane processes, which is often presented as a major advantage, has been somehow forgotten in the previous statement.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Physical gas separation membranes
texts.text: 4.2. Physical gas separation membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membranes based on a physical separation principle (i.e. without any chemical reaction) are already used at industrial scale for different applications [34]. Polymers are almost systematically proposed in order to achieve the adequate trade-off between selectivity and permeability. According to the solution-diffusion model, which can be considered as the cornerstone in dense polymeric membranes separations, the separation factor will depend on a kinetic term (namely ratio of the diffusion coefficients of the two species in the membrane) and a thermodynamic term (the ratio of the solubilities of the two compounds in the polymeric matrix) [35]. For the CO2/N2 mixture, both experimental results [36-40] and theoretical computations [41] come to the conclusion that a maximal selectivity of 70-80 can be achieved. Table 3 summarizes some of the most efficient membranes which have been recently reported, for which the selectivity limit is approached or attained. It is interesting to notice that a similar value is obtained for physical solvents in gas-liquid absorption processes for CO2/N2 under atmospheric pressure. Coming back to Fig. 7, it can be concluded that the classical coal power plant flue gas treatment will not be feasible in a single membrane stage under these conditions (for ˛ = 75, it is impossible to get a capture ratio R = 0.8 together with purity y = 0.9).

texts.orig: Consequently, classical gas separation membrane processes demand more than one separation stage in order to reach the specifications. Different studies have explored the possibilities and associated performances of multistage membrane processes in a post-combustion situation [42-47]. Dry model CO2/N2 mixtures have been almost systematically taken as feed mixtures. The situation is far from being completely analysed in terms of process design options (number of stages, compression vs. vacuum operation, purge sweep, partial recycling, . . . ). Improvement in membrane permeance appears to be of importance in order to limit the membrane surface area (and consequently the capital cost of the process [44,47]). Nevertheless, with already existing materials with a selectivity of 50 and a permeance of 1000 GPU, CO2 capture costs as low as 23 $/ton has been estimated [47]. It is expected that the efforts recently made by different companies in this field, particularly the MTR first pilot plant operation on a flue gas with gas separation membranes [47], will provide a sound feedback on the possibilities and limitations of membrane processes for postcombustion applications.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.4. Liquid membranes
texts.text: 4.4. Liquid membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Liquid membranes have been proposed several decades ago for CO2/N2 separation for space applications, and extremely high selectivities (up to 4500) have been reported [18]. A large number of studies have been performed in the meanwhile on different systems [53-56], and a few of them are summarized in Table 3. To some extent, liquid membranes share common features with FSCM from a practical point of view: a very high selectivity can be attained, the effective permeance of the membranes under real operating conditions is difficult to estimate and stability over long time scale is a key issue. Given the difficulties encountered when a volatile solvent is used for carrier dissolution (such as water), different low boiling liquids have been tested (e.g. glycerol); in that context, ionic liquids appear to be very promising [57,58], but further proof of concepts studies are needed, together with pilot experiments, in order to estimate the real potential of this technology.

texts.orig: 5.1. CO2 sources
texts.text: 5.1. CO2 sources

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane processes offer increased separation performances when CO2 concentration in the feed mixture increases. This very high parametric sensitivity can best be observed when the data sets which enable a 0.9 purity to be obtained with a 15% CO2 feed mixture (Fig. 7) are compared to the 30% CO2 feed mixture case (Fig. 8). The coal power plant reference case (ca 15 vol.% CO2 content) clearly represents difficult conditions for membrane processes. Comparatively, a 30% CO2 feed mixture shows a much more favourable context: with a CO2/N2 selectivity of 75 or more, a 80% capture ratio ( R ) together with a 0.9 purity ( y ) can be attained at a reasonable pressure ratio ( ∼ 0.1). Consequently emissions where a CO2 volume fraction of 0.2 or more should be explored as the most favourable application niche for membranes. These will correspond to smaller tonnages compared to the coal power plant sector, but significant applications can be found in this category. For instance, biofuels, sodium carbonate, ammonia, syngas production can lead to high CO2 content in the emissions. Biogas appears also to be very promising [59].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Up to now the search of selectivity membrane materials has been restricted to CO2 selective structures. Reverse selective CO2/N2 membrane materials would be extremely appealing, because they would minimize the CO2 capture energy requirement and let the concentrated CO2 on the high pressure side, a favourable situation before compression and transport. Unfortunately, apart from microporous materials for which a limited Knudsen N2/CO2 selectivity can be obtained ( ˛ N2/CO2 ∼ 1.12 [60]), reverse selective materials are unfortunately unavailable today and they are probably unlikely to be obtained through the classical molecular separation mechanisms that prevail in membrane science [61].

texts.orig: The analysis and evaluation of the energy requirement and the energy efficiency of different membrane processes is also an important issue. It has been stressed that this issue, shown in Fig. 2, is the most stringent capture process selection criteria. It is striking however that opposite opinions, from very efficient [71] to extremely inefficient [72], can be found on the energy efficiency performances of membrane processes. More specifically, the selection of a pressurized feed stream or vacuum pumping on the permeate side for postcombustion capture still remains an open question. To that respect, pressure drop considerations both in the permeate and feed side have not been investigated in detail up to now.

texts.orig: high H2O/CO2 selectivities (typically several thousands) can be obtained. This could offer interesting potential for oxycombustion, where a CO2/H2O mixture is produced. Surprisingly, studies on flue gas dehydration by membranes remain scarce [66]. Another more generic problem concerns the influence of water on membrane performances, especially the changes on CO2 permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity in the presence of humidity. Very few investigations on multicomponent CO2/N2/H2O mixtures have been reported and very different conclusions can be drawn, depending on the type of membrane. For example, humidity can induce a spectacular decrease of CO2 permeability (e.g. for polyetherimide [67] and polyimide membranes [68]), an increase [69] or a globally unchanged [70] CO2/N2 selectivity. A better understanding of the influence of water on CO2 and N2 permeation is of absolute necessity for the most relevant place and role of membrane processes to be defined in a post-combustion situation.

texts.orig: -The fate of the other, 'minor' species listed in Table 4 in membrane processes is almost completely unexplored up to now. A series of trade-off curves has been calculated based on a method developed by Freeman [35], in order to evaluate the possible enrichment or rejection of the different species compared to CO2 permeation, and the results are shown in Fig. 9. A comparison between the trade-off limit curve and a series of permeation data for the CO2/O2 gas pair is also shown in Fig. 10 in order to evaluate a comparions between experimental data and the trade-off limit predictions. Taking the CO2/N2 gas pair as a reference, it can be concluded from Fig. 9 that, apart from CO which is slower than CO2, all other species will show a separation selectivity which is lower than that of CO2/N2. Given the large variety of slopes that are generated for the different gas pairs, a large range of permeate compositions can result. The case of O2, which is often present in the percent range in flue gas, seems to be particularly unfavorable, if a very low concentration (ppm level) can be tolerated in permeate (Table 4).

texts.orig: 6. Conclusion
texts.text: 6. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The objective of this paper was to provide a state of the art analysis of different membrane processes for post-combustion carbon dioxide capture, in order to identify forthcoming research needs. Various research directions, corresponding to different membrane processes and/or target applications have been tentatively exposed and discussed. The landscape shows a large variety of situations, from CO2 sources to capture process requirements and polishing operations, which could offer space for a given membrane process. Several issues have to be clarified however before the most relevant place and role of membranes is identified. Generally speaking, there is scope for more research on:
texts.text: The objective of this paper was to provide a state of the art analysis of different membrane processes for post-combustion carbon dioxide capture, in order to identify forthcoming research needs. Various research directions, corresponding to different membrane processes and/or target applications have been tentatively exposed and discussed. The landscape shows a large variety of situations, from CO2 sources to capture process requirements and polishing operations, which could offer space for a given membrane process. Several issues have to be clarified however before the most relevant place and role of membranes is identified. Generally speaking, there is scope for more research on:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -Studies which combine materials and engineering aspects . This association has always been the key driver of membrane processes and the carbon capture challenge demands these two facets to be rigorously and simultaneously treated.

A large effort on technicoeconomical studies with membrane processes which rigorously take into account these different objectives is necessary. For oxygen production for instance, reliable cost curves are available, which allows the potential of a given material and/or process specifications to be quickly estimated. table_cells.text: Membrane stability (non wetting conditions) has to be ensured for long time operation Regeneration step non achievable Effective intensification factor remains to be clearly evaluated
1. Gas separation membranes: physical (GS) (usually a dense polymer)

Numerous materials investigated, few technico-economical studies, almost no pilot scale process on real flue gas Water in flue gas could be a problem Require multistage processes in order to achieve purity target

Require water, on both sides of the membrane, to be effective May be applicable through a single stage process if a high selectivity is attained Industrial feedback on real flue gas still not achieved

Very high selectivity can be achieved Require water, on both sides of the membrane to be effective Solvent losses due to volatility can be a problem, possibly solved by ionic liquids Effective driving force, stability and effective permeability in a real post-combustion situation remain to be proven
Flue gas or concentrated CO 2 drying

Water will always permeate faster than CO 2 The incidence of water permeation on CO 2 permeability depends on polymer type Competitiveness compared to other operation (absorption molecular sieves) remains to be proven
The fate of minor species (O 2 , NO x , SO x , . . . ) is a key issue, still largely unexplored
